text: The surgeon performs a left hepatectomy to address bleeding hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), focusing on the resection of the left liver lobe.
The surgical team has made a right subcostal incision with a midline extension, providing access to the liver for the hepatectomy procedure.
The Makuchi is in season.
The surgical field reveals active bleeding from the tumor site, with visible old clots surrounding the area.
The video shows an active bleed from the tumor during the left hepatectomy procedure for bleeding hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
The surgeon has brought down the gallbladder, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct, preparing to dissect these structures.
The surgeon ligates the cystic duct following the ligation of the cystic artery, and proceeds to remove the gallbladder from its anatomical position.
The surgical field shows continuous bleeding from the tumor, with blood visibly collecting in the area.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the hepatic artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to ligate the hepatic artery and portal vein to control the bleeding from the tumor.
The surgeon identifies the left hepatic artery, which is ligated, clipped, and being divided. The right hepatic artery is also visible in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows persistent bleeding from the tumor, with blood visibly collecting in the area despite previous ligations.
The surgeon continues the dissection around the porta hepatis to identify the right hepatic artery and confirm the artery supplying segment four, preparing to ligate it.
The surgeon loops the portal vein, preparing it for ligation as part of the liver resection procedure.
The surgeon has ligated and divided the middle hepatic artery, also known as the artery to segment four, with visible clips securing the vessel.

The surgeon mobilizes the portal vein, clipping its tributary to segment four. The left branch of the portal vein is looped using a blue sling, while the right branch is clearly identified. The portal vein is then double ligated and divided, leaving the remnant end visible.
The surgeon performs an oversewing of the portal vein using a proline stitch, applying a continuous double-layer closure technique to the left portal vein branch.
The surgeon removes the clamp from the portal vein to check for active bleeding before cutting the proline suture. If bleeding is observed, additional stitches are applied to control it.
The surgical field appears increasingly dry, indicating successful control of bleeding following the ligation and division of the portal vein and middle hepatic artery.
The surgical field shows no evidence of active bleeding as the inflow to the vessel has been successfully ligated.
The surgeon divides the left triangular ligament and mobilizes the left lobe of the liver, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the line of demarcation for the parenchymal transection on the liver surface, marking the area where the liver tissue will be divided.
The surgeon places two stay sutures and begins dissection using a harmonic scalpel, focusing on precise tissue separation.
The surgeon identifies the vein leading to segment five of the liver and follows its course towards the inferior vena cava (IVC), ensuring clear visualization of the vascular pathway.
These were the settings with CVPF six.
We were going ahead.
The surgeon defines the metaphylactic vein, tracing its course following the segment five vein towards the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon proceeds with the parenchymal transection of the liver using a combination of harmonic scalpel, clips, sutures, and calipers.
The surgeon follows the middle hepatic vein, tracing its path within the liver tissue.
I think in this situation CUSA would have been a better to follow the middle hepatic vein towards the IVC.
The cut surface of the liver appears irregular and ragged, likely due to the parenchymal division technique used, highlighting the visual difference compared to smoother surfaces achieved with CUSA.
The surgeon has ligated the segment IV branch and is now proceeding with dissection along the middle hepatic vein.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from the middle hepatic vein during parenchymal transection by using forceps to grasp the vessel, applying suction to dry the area, and placing a suture with proline to control the bleeding.
The surgeon uses a multiplier or clips to control hemostasis around the middle hepatic vein, ensuring minimal bleeding during the liver resection procedure.
The surgeon ligates and cuts the vessels, resulting in the opening of more liver parenchyma, revealing the internal structure of the liver.
The surgeon double clips and ligates the segment four A branch as it approaches the middle hepatic vein, then proceeds to divide the vessel.
The surgeon continues the parenchymal transduction, carefully separating liver tissue while managing any bleeding from the middle hepatic vein using forceps and suction.
The surgeon applies a clamp on the left hepatic vein, visibly occluding the vessel to control blood flow during the liver resection.
The specimen, removed after the left hepatectomy, is displayed, showing the excised liver tissue.
The surgeon places a multipulverated romodrain near the cut surface of the liver following the resection.
The surgeon applies a hemostatic glue to the cut surface of the liver to control bleeding.
